draft.introduction:
Republic 473c–d contains the most famous—and most controversial—political proposal in Western philosophy. Socrates declares that cities will never cease from their evils until philosophers become kings or kings become philosophers. The claim is not rhetorical. Plato means it literally: only those who have seen the Form of the Good are qualified to govern, because only they understand what justice actually is rather than merely what it appears to be.

The passage arrives at the dramatic climax of Book V, where Socrates has been progressively raising the stakes of his ideal city. He knows the proposal will provoke ridicule—he calls it the “third wave” of paradox, the one most likely to drown him in laughter and disbelief. The philosopher-king argument is inseparable from Plato’s metaphysics: if there are eternal Forms accessible only to trained reason, then governance by the untrained is governance by the blind. Democracy, in this framework, is not liberation but the rule of ignorance over knowledge.

translation.text: Unless either philosophers become kings in their cities, or those who are now called kings and rulers come to be sufficiently and genuinely philosophizing, and this—political power and philosophy—coincide in the same person, while the many natures who at present pursue either one exclusively are forcibly prevented from doing so, there will be no rest from evils for the cities, my dear Glaucon, nor I think for the human race.
translation.original_text: Ἐὰν μή, ἦν δ’ ἐγώ, ἢ οἱ φιλόσοφοι βασιλεύσωσιν ἐν ταῖς πόλεσιν ἢ οἱ βασιλεῖς τε νῦν λεγόμενοι καὶ δυνάσται γνησίως τε καὶ ἱκανῶς φιλοσοφήσωσι, καὶ τοῦτο εἰς ταὐτὸν συμπέσῃ, δύναμίς τε πολιτικὴ καὶ φιλοσοφία, τῶν δὲ νῦν πορευομένων χωρὶς ἐφ’ ἑκάτερον αἱ πολλαὶ φύσεις ἐξ ἀνάγκης ἀποκλεισθῶσιν, οὐκ ἔστι κακῶν παῦλα, ὦ φίλε Γλαύκων, ταῖς πόλεσιν, δοκῶ δ’ οὐδὲ τῷ ἀνθρωπίνῳ γένει.

translation.text: Nor will this constitution that we have been describing in our discourse ever previously grow to the extent that is possible and see the light of the sun, until this very thing comes to pass. This is what has for so long made me hesitate to speak, because I saw that saying it would be very much against the grain of common opinion. For it is difficult to see that in no other city could there be happiness, either public or private.
translation.original_text: οὐδὲ αὕτη ἡ πολιτεία ἥν νῦν τῷ λόγῳ διεληλύθαμεν πρότερον φύσει εἰς τὸ δυνατὸν αὐξηθεῖσα φῶς ἡλίου ὄψεται.

translator_notes.note: The Greek φιλόσοφοι here does not mean “intellectuals” or “academics” in the modern sense. For Plato, the philosopher is one who has undergone the rigorous training described in Books VI–VII—dialectic, mathematics, and ultimately the vision of the Good—and who returns unwillingly to govern out of duty rather than ambition. The philosopher-king rules not because he desires power but precisely because he does not.
